files.SKILL.md:
Read `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md` and produce the deliverable at the specified path. Do not declare done until the deliverable exists and the system behaves as the brief specifies.

## Locating the failure

When the brief quotes a concrete error message, stack trace, or unexpected output, that string is your single most valuable starting point. A repository-wide search for it usually narrows the failing branch to one or two locations. Read enough surrounding context to understand what input reaches that branch and what alternative branch the brief expects to be taken instead.

## Inspecting before modifying

When working with stateful artifacts that share their state across multiple files (a primary plus sidecars, manifests, lock files, logs), opening the primary with the standard tool often has side-effects on the sidecars. Copy each sidecar aside before invoking any tool that opens the primary, so destructive side-effects are reversible.

When binary content looks wrong, the first few bytes of any structured format are a fixed magic number distinct for each format. Comparing observed bytes against the format's published magic at each offset reveals whether the file is shifted, truncated, or transformed by a single-byte or short-period operation. The inverse of any byte-wise transformation is the same operation; apply it and read again.

## Bounded concurrency with safe cancellation

In cooperative concurrency systems, eager submission of every work item as a long-lived future, throttled by a shared resource, has a subtle failure shape on cancellation: items still queued on the resource are full-blown future objects whose own cancellation cleanup competes with the cancellation of the in-flight items, and the in-flight ones never get to run their finalization branches.

The robust shape is bounded submission: process work items in groups of the concurrency cap, never holding more outstanding futures than the cap permits. Items beyond the current group are not yet futures, so there is nothing in flight to interfere with cancellation of the running ones.

## When the deliverable changes a real system

Scripts that change machine state (install packages, edit configurations, start services) run in environments whose conveniences you cannot assume. Two assumptions break most often: the elevation tool you reach for may not be installed, and the service-management tool may require infrastructure you don't have. Reach for the lowest-common-denominator alternative — invoke daemon binaries by absolute path, use built-in coreutils for user-switching rather than third-party elevation, and keep an exit-code-tolerant fallback chain that ends at the binary directly.

When non-root processes need write access at runtime, the directory must be owned by that user — not by whoever ran the install script. Pair every directory creation with an explicit ownership change at install time, so the deferred runtime write succeeds.

Run the system's own syntax-check facility before declaring done. A syntactically valid configuration that fails to load is a self-evident debug hint; a syntactically invalid one means the artifact would not even be tried.

## Reproducing an existing artifact

When the brief provides a sample output and asks for a program that regenerates it, the reference was made by a specific procedure with specific parameters. The sample's header carries dimensions, format magic, and value range. The sample's body carries shape and parameter signatures: pixel gradients reveal lighting direction, edges reveal object placement, repeated structures reveal palette and tiling. Measure before guessing.

If the brief constrains the source size (in absolute or compressed bytes), the constraint forces algorithmic generation rather than data embedding. Shorter identifiers, inline operations, and trimmed instrumentation usually fit comfortably.

## When the test contract lives in the source tree

If the source tree includes the tests that grade the failing behavior, open them. The contract is broader than "the input now produces the right output" — it usually also requires specific warnings, specific exceptions, exact return shapes, or specific captured streams. Skim the test before designing your change so the change satisfies the whole contract, not just the surface symptom.

When a test wraps a call in a warnings-assertion context, the wrapped code must emit a warning of the asserted class. A change that silently makes the input work, without emitting the expected warning, fails the contract.

## Validating before declaring done

A change you have not actually executed is hypothetical. Run the modified code with the brief's failing input and with a previously-working input. Watch actual output: warnings emitted, return values, side effects. Both inputs must succeed; if either does not, the change is incomplete.

For deliverables that drive other processes (shell scripts, build files, deploy manifests), the target system's own validation facility (syntax check, dry-run, lint) is the ground truth — observed output beats mental tracing.

Re-read your final deliverable once. Does the file exist at the named path with the asked-for structure? Does every change reference a real line in the named source? Are previously-passing behaviors preserved? Is the structural shape (list-of-records, CSV header, ordering, deduplication) exactly what the brief specifies?
